raw_payload.author: MorfenZ
raw_payload.content:
Crime 101 left me with genuinely mixed emotions. The cast is one of the film's strongest assets - Chris Hemsworth, Halle Berry, and Mark Ruffalo all deliver committed performances, and you can feel their effort in every scene. The core idea of the story is intriguing, and there's real potential in its themes and character dynamics. Visually, the film has quality; some moments are shot beautifully and carry a strong cinematic presence.

However, the biggest weakness for me lies in the writing and direction. The tension-building sequences feel overstretched and forced, and instead of creating suspense, they become exhausting. I often felt tired rather than invested during crucial moments. The protagonist's character arc is also uneven - he starts as a disciplined, rule-driven man, but his shift in behavior feels unearned and poorly developed.

Overall, Crime 101 isn't a bad film, but its inconsistent writing and dragged pacing hold it back from becoming the gripping crime drama it could have been.

raw_payload.author: somf
raw_payload.content:
Caught an early screening tonight of the almost flawless Crime 101. The title sounds pedestrian but the film is anything but. I have seen the Imposter which I like and American Animals which I gave 4 of 5 stars to. They are two of director Bart Layton's earlier films, but this film is a big step up.

I think Don Winslow is one of the best writers alive today. I have read most of his books but never read his short stories. This is adapted from Winslow's short story. This film is going to properly be compared to Michael Mann's films, but for me I also thought about Sidney Lumet, whose body of work is rarely talked about today but who never made a bad film. Both director's made smart adult films. Solid acting, solid scripts, and in the Mann films an incredible soundtracks creating the perfect mood. There is an intensity that runs throughout Crime 101. It may be cliche to say it keeps you on the edge of your seat, but there is not a wasted frame. Hemsworth has never been better and Ruffalo is starting to remind me a lot of Columbo these days, but he too does a fantastic job. Halle Barry is excellent as always with her using every year of her life on this earth to make her character so rich. And then there is Barry Keoghan. He is incredible in yet another role as total psycho. Nick Nolte has a small role. This kind of film was his forte in the 80's including one I had not thought about for years called Q and A that made me first think of Lumet. I hope this does great at the box office, because I need Layton to tackle Winslow's best novel "Power of the Dog" next.(no relation to the western film a few years back) There will be some negative reviews about the film being derivative or slow, but for those of us with an attention span long enough to appreciate a solid plot over a series of super hero set pieces, this is a perfect film.

They don't make films like this much anymore. Go see it so maybe they will make more.

raw_payload.author: kjproulx
raw_payload.content:
I'm a sucker for a good cops-and-robbers story, so Crime 101 was immediately on my radar. I was also a big fan of American Animals, so seeing Bart Layton in the director's chair made me even more curious. On top of that, the cast is absolutely stacked, which made it feel like all the ingredients were there for something special. Thankfully, the film delivers on most of that promise. While I don't quite think it reaches the level of fantastic, it is still a very good and engaging crime thriller that kept me invested from start to finish.

The story follows Davis (Chris Hemsworth), a high-end thief who makes his living by being patient and strategic. Like most criminals, though, his careful routine starts to slip. He begins a relationship with Maya (Monica Barbaro) and takes a risky step by inserting himself into the life of Sharon (Halle Berry), all while trying to quietly exit the game. Meanwhile, Detective Lou (Mark Ruffalo) starts to notice patterns in Davis' work and becomes determined to bring him down. With a few twists along the way and another robber entering the mix, played by Barry Keoghan, the narrative always had something interesting happening and never lost my attention.

Visually, this is a sleek and sharp film that feels very polished. The cinematography is stylish, the direction is confident, and the overall tone fits the story perfectly. I liked the characters, their motivations, and where the story ultimately goes. My biggest issue, however, is the pacing. At two hours and twenty minutes, the film feels longer than it needs to be. There are several scenes that could have been tightened or trimmed, and I truly believe that cutting it down to just under two hours would have made it an even stronger film. It's never boring, but it definitely lingers in places where it didn't have to.

The performances are a major highlight across the board, with the cast bringing a lot of weight to the material. The score is subtle but effective, rising at just the right moments, and Layton's direction keeps the tension steady throughout. The editing is solid for the most part, though it could have been sharper in certain stretches to help the pacing. Overall, Crime 101 is a slick, well-acted crime thriller that I had a great time watching. It's a very good movie that I'd easily recommend to fans of the genre, even if it runs a bit longer than necessary.

raw_payload.author: garygwilliams
raw_payload.content: This is a well-constructed heist movie with healthy dose of the human condition. I like the performances overall, but star actors should stop getting Barry Keoghan as a supporting character as he is a bona fide scene stealer. He is my favorite wackadoo. The convenience store scene is indelibly etched in my brain. It has a better than most chase scene of that brings back memories of Bullitt and the French Connection. The whole thing seemed like a throwback to the 60s and 70s. Enjoyable and my favorite so far this year.

raw_payload.author: Katiegoldberg
raw_payload.content:
I walked out of 'Crime 101' buzzing. The trailer really doesn't do it justice at all. This is a sleek, stylish crime thriller that knows exactly what it's doing and does it with confidence. From the first frame it feels expensive in the best way, all cool surfaces, sun soaked tension and that irresistible slow burn energy where you know something's going to snap.

The performances are rock solid across the board and the cinematography is genuinely gorgeous. LA has never looked so seductive and so threatening at the same time. Are there moments where you have to suspend your disbelief? Yes. Absolutely. But does it matter when you're having this much fun? Also yes, but in the way where you happily go along for the ride because the film has earned your trust.

What really ties it all together is the score. It's sharp, propulsive and adds a constant undercurrent of tension that keeps you locked in even during the quieter scenes. 'Crime 101' isn't trying to reinvent the genre, but it polishes it to a high gloss and delivers a hugely entertaining, very cool couple of hours. Stylish, smart and just a great time at the cinema.

raw_payload.author: ahmdgabr
raw_payload.content:
Crime 101 starts strong. The first half builds tension well, and I appreciated that Chris Hemsworth wasn't written as the usual invincible action hero. His vulnerability made the character more grounded and interesting.

But the second half loses focus.

Too many coincidences drive the plot, and the ending feels unrealistic - especially with several characters committing serious crimes and facing no real consequences. It feels like the writer avoided taking risks and chose the safer route.

For a movie marketed as action, there's also surprisingly little action, particularly when the story should have escalated.

The biggest disappointment is the mysterious past. It's built up as something major, yet the film ends without fully revealing or resolving it, leaving the character arc incomplete.

Overall, it's not a bad film - just one that doesn't live up to its own potential. 6.5/10.

raw_payload.author: Billy_Boy_
raw_payload.content:
Crime 101 clearly wants to be in the same league as Heat and Thief. You can feel that influence in the moody synth score, the coastal night photography and the quiet standoffs between cops and criminals. Sometimes it works. Sometimes it just feels too much like imitation.

Chris Hemsworth plays against type as a more introverted thief. I appreciate the attempt, but he never fully disappears into the role. You still see the movie star. Mark Ruffalo brings the most weight to the film.

The first half builds tension well. However, the second half leans too much on coincidences and soft resolutions. Big reveals land with a shrug.

It looks slick and sounds great, but never quite grips you the way it should, making it solid and watchable, just not particularly memorable.

raw_payload.author: Zhenderzee
raw_payload.content:
On paper it should be a knock out banger of a heist film instead of one that packs less of what it's Age rating would suggest or the amount of action you'd expect from a crime drama. The story is split into three PoVs with three characters, the Thief, the Insurer and the Detective. Neither of the three are particularly likeable by the end despite some moral moments but they all end up getting their pockets lined or outright get away with serious crimes with a slap on the wrist.

All three protagonists have a stack of side characters that end up vanishing from the plot randomly. The Carrier-business man getting polygraphed to get his insurance claim sunk has no aftermath as he vanished after the beginning with the Insurer saying nothing about the result, the Thief gets to go through three women with completely different personalities as if the writers couldn't nail the character Hemsworth is supposed to play. The second of which gave him a chance in their romance despite being so secretive and isolated that accepting red flags after another makes her a joke. The Insurer gets moments of rattling on soapboxes but Crime 101 makes no attempt of showing the aftermath and skip right past it. While the Detective remained clean until he became dirty as everyone else by magically knowing what the Thief promised the Insurer and lining the latter's pockets in the end.

The pacing, writing and action is poor. The atmosphere and acting while decent at a good chunk of the scenes. Characters during action scenes end up throwing repetitive threats at each other with no attention since all the protagonists have plot armour and the chases being so slow that characters end up letting their targets prolong the chase just to extend the movie run time.

The choice of words is often questionable and slang ridden as I never want to see Hemsworth try to talk to the elderly Ringleader that hired him about Homies and Homeboys ever again. It felt more like an attempt to make characters be more trendy chase younger audiences that wouldn't fit with the Age rating in the first place. The Ringleader also being the one behind all the Heists as the mastermind completely vanished midway as his Robber henchman completely took over as the Thief's replacement and Amy mention of him is all but forgotten as the movie concludes.

Many plot contrivances also exist with so many ways the characters could fail in their actions but because of a long line of coincidences, they managed to keep the plot rolling. The movie told the audience how careful and prepared the Thief was in executing his crimes but every time you see him on screen, he acts careless and incompetent to the point that there are huge bread crumbs for the Detective to follow. An example being the damaged car he used in the opening Heist but never disposed off, just left in a random parking lot with a tarp on it just waiting to be found, pickup calls without a doubt it make an attempt to not get tailed to name a few examples.

His origins as well was painted as deeper than it really is, a barely explained rags to riches story leading him to go through women like clothes until he chose one that only the plot decides he would get endless chances to continue his relationship with. Many times the movie teased that the Lover would be just as deep or mysterious like a rival Thief in a Heist team up like the Oceans or Now you See Me franchises but no, she was as ordinary and accepting as a generic love interest is. Wasting all the potential.

For a star studded cast, the quality of writing is anything but.

raw_payload.author: FeastMode
raw_payload.content:
All jokes aside, this is an all-star cast. All four are highly talented with great screen presence. It's great to see beast Halle Berry again. And Barry Keoghan plays such a wild and memorable character (more about his performance than depth in the writing).

This is a classic crime thriller. It does a great job building tension and executing suspense. There are lots of moving parts but it's easy to follow. I enjoyed the musical score and the pretty transition shots. And there are some cool little details that show me they care.

For example, in an early scene (not a spoiler), a character removes colored contact lenses. This plays no role in the story and could have been removed without affecting anything. But it conveys the level of detail and planning by the character, to change something he could be identified by, the color of his eyes (he was wearing a mask).

I only had a couple small issues with the movie. One small plot thread is left unresolved. I was hoping for a satisfying payoff. And I don't want this to sound mean, but it felt like Nick Nolte was struggling to be there and read his lines. It took me out of the movie.

I had fun time with Crime 101. I was invested throughout and would watch it again.

(1 viewing, early screening 2/9/2026)

raw_payload.author: Binkconn
raw_payload.content: This is a great kind of crime thriller no one makes anymore, of intersecting L. A. professionals at a crossroads in their lives: Hemsworth the jewel thief whose nerves are starting to fray, Halle the aging insurance adjuster trying to keep up with the hot up-and-comers, Ruffalo continuing his slow transformation into Peter Falk as a divorced detective sorting out the crimes. Good character scenes, great use of the city's diverse neighborhoods, intense chases...when Wuthering Heights starts to get a bit tiresome check this one out.

raw_payload.author: johnfuen-42506
raw_payload.content:
Honestly, I liked it more than I expected to.

It's not one of those loud, over-the-top crime movies. It's more mysterious and controlled. The story was actually good - not super predictable, and it kept me guessing without feeling confusing.

The vibe was steady the whole time. Not chaotic, not boring. Just this low simmer that builds.

And the ending sequence with Chris Hemsworth and Mark Ruffalo? That actually had me on the edge of my seat. I don't even remember the last time I felt that locked in during a theater scene. It was tense in a good way - like you knew something big was about to happen but couldn't fully see how it would play out.

Overall I'd give it like a 7.5/10.

Not a mind-blowing masterpiece, but definitely worth seeing. Solid story, solid tension, and it actually made me feel something at the end - which is rare.

raw_payload.author: Assassin8all
raw_payload.content: I wanted to like this more. And that's not to say it was a bad crime thriller. In fact, it was really good. I just think that pacing was its biggest issue. During some bits, the story repeats itself and the same points are repeated. And the 2 hour 2 minute run time does not do it justice. Although, I still really enjoyed this film. The character development want bad. Chris Hemsworth was a really believable good guy, wrong job, kind of role. Halle Berry, wow! Her performance in this film stood out for me, don't know if that's just me. Mark Ruffalo and Hemsworth needed more screen time. Pretty good first crime thriller of the year.

raw_payload.author: waltermwilliams
raw_payload.content:
If I've learned one thing from going to the cinema nearly my whole life it's "always have an exit".

While I love a good heist movie "Crime 101" didn't know where to get off!

The good news is you'll see some awesome action car chases, because, "life's too short to drive boring cars".

The bad news is the bits in between tend to drag on longer than the 101 highway in California.

Fun Fact: It's 2,478 km!

Stay tuned for the Steve McQueen references which sum up what "Crime 101" was aiming for and fell just short of.

Missed it by that much!

I found the script highly predictable and it all panned out as I expected, but it's offset by a plethora of Marvel & DC actors and that's always a positive.

Chris Hemsworth shows some real depth in his poor foster child turns rich criminal "Davis".

Mark Ruffalo's old, beefy cop "Lou" with a hunch was cliche, but believable.

Overlooked for promotions in an ageist, misogynistic insurance company, Halle Berry felt genuine as "Sharon"...may as well have been Karen.

Unpredictable as always is the unhinged, peroxide, biker thief with crazy eyes Barry Keoghan ("Saltburn) as "Ormon" who stole all his scenes.

Sadly the legendary Nick Nolte as the crime boss "Money", doesn't sound well, it was like watching Val Kilmer in his last Top Gun movie.

You know a movie has pulling power when it includes one academy award winner and four nominees including Jennifer Jason Leigh who only gets one scene.

Listen out for: a cool soundtrack featuring Bryan Adams and Bruce Springsteen.

This generic caper flick was directed and written by Bart Layton and while it's no "Heat"it has redeeming features that make it an entertaining way to spend over 2 hours.

If you're still keen to see this crime procedural film it's coming to Prime next month who were smart enough to give it a theatrical release after a bidding war with Netflix.

"Crime 101" deserves a big screen viewing first.

raw_payload.author: Sprite_Lee
raw_payload.content:
When you hear the phrase 101, you assume you're getting an introduction to a course or a specific subject. But this is L. A., the City of Angels, where 101 usually means traffic, depending on the time of day.

Crime 101 is a heist drama that delivers with its A-list celebrity cast and shines because of some of their performances. Where the film lacks, they make up for it with strong acting. Obviously, Ruffalo and Hemsworth have a lot of on-screen chemistry together, but when you add the talents of Halle Berry and Barry Keoghan, you get the sense that something good is going on.

But it pretty much stops there for me. I don't believe the stakes, the motivation, or the threat. Either it doesn't feel real, or they're nonexistent altogether. The film is a very slow burn that has its moments, but due to some inconsistencies and a lack of tension, I found myself only partially engaged in the drama. Characters are introduced who were blatantly crowbarred into the film and don't really serve any purpose other than to play into movie tropes, while others are so forgettable you barely remember they exist.

At no point did I find myself lost in the story, rooting for the lead, or even understanding why or how he fell into this life of crime. I feel that the film would've benefited from a shorter runtime, given that some scenes and dialogue felt like filler and weren't really necessary to the story. It's a watch where you just have to go with it. Even with the limited action and suspense, I can see audiences enjoying it. It's just simply not for me.

raw_payload.author: HabibieHakim123
raw_payload.content:
It's unnecessarily long, complicated, and simply bored the hell out of me, i haven't been in that level of bored on a movie in quite some time, both of the performance and characters are basically lifeless, what's frustrating is that the story could've been told in a much simpler, more focused way, instead, it piles on event after event, subplot after subplot, until it feels bloated.

Halle Berry's character, for example, seems important mainly because of her connection to Chris Hemsworth's target, yet much of her screen time feels unimportant, Mark Ruffalo doesn't bring much charm to the role either, Hemsworth, as the lead, comes across surprisingly flat, lacking the presence you'd expect, Keoghan is just wandering around being a boring maniac, and Nick Nolte is just weird.

Most of the main performances are decent at best, but since the characters are so lifeless, it's hard to care about any of them, the only aspects that truly stand out are the visual style, which at least maintains a distinct look, and Monica Barbaro, who delivers the strongest work in the film.

In the end, i'm left wondering why the story chose to complicate itself so much, there was a solid idea here, and it could have worked, instead, it becomes exhausting and, ultimately, boring, i hate it.

raw_payload.author: advikrai
raw_payload.content:
Went in expecting a worse version of Heat. I was pleasantly surprised with how well made this was. Every one was amazing in their roles, although Hemsworth's accent wasn't too convincing, his acting made up for it. Overall almost everything about this movie was GOOD, but it could have been Great, alas. Unexpectedly a very fun movie.

Will recommend.

7.5/10.

raw_payload.author: sandgroper-it
raw_payload.content:
Crime 101 is the cinematic equivalent of a luxury sports car with no engine - sleek exterior, big-name badge, and absolutely nowhere to go.

On paper, this should've been a modern classic. The cast lineup screams prestige crime thriller. The marketing promises something in the realm of Heat, The Score, or Absolute Power - high stakes, sharp tension, professional criminals playing chess against relentless law enforcement.

Instead, what you get is a hollow, sluggish film that mistakes mood for momentum.

The problem isn't the acting. The problem is that the movie never earns its own seriousness. It wants to feel like a smart, adult thriller, but it's mostly just people talking in low tones while the plot drifts around hoping atmosphere will substitute for urgency.

Where Heat builds pressure until it becomes unbearable... Where The Score is clever and tightly engineered... Where Absolute Power actually feels dangerous... Crime 101 just sort of... exists.

There's no escalation. No pulse. No real sense that anything matters. And so many loose ends it doesn't wrap up well.

It's almost impressive how a film with this many talented people manages to feel so weightless.

The trailer sells a tense, high-stakes cat-and-mouse crime epic. The actual movie plays more like an overlong streaming pilot that forgot to develop tension, payoff, or memorable set pieces.

Honestly, The Rookie - a network TV show - delivers better pacing, personality, and thriller energy in 45 minutes than Crime 101 manages in two hours.

This film is oversold, underwritten, and ultimately forgettable. Not worth the dollars to go to the cinema, but a cheap streaming flick to kill an evening.

A crime thriller with no thrill is just a slow walk in a nice suit. Basically it's one of Chris Hemsworths worst movies, as it plays to absolutely none of Chris' charms, personality or style.

All style, no stakes.

raw_payload.author: Bruhsup
raw_payload.content:
The movie had a strong beginning and had me invested.

I loved the beginning heist and the car chase towards the middle. Scenes with Ormon were the best part, but he wasn't in the movie much. And Davis also seemed like an interesting character but wasn't fleshed out much.

Though, as the movie kept going, a lot of the issues began to unveil themselves.

For starters, there's just so many stupid decisions that Davis oversteps when realizing he's getting replaced, like how did he not notice that Ormon was at the diner? He clearly should be keeping an eye out on the motorcyclist who stole his heist plans. The motorcyclist who's clearly in the parking lot. Not to mention Davis completely ignored the fact that Ormon tailed Sharon, yet gave her no protection whatsoever.

Also wouldn't his former boss know about these plans as well? Why didn't Davis take precautions just in case Ormon got replaced with someone else after threatening him?

A lot of the characters also felt underdeveloped. You had these subplots for each of the main characters with Shannon's work status being the only one getting a bit fleshed out while the police corruption and Davis's ex-business partner don't.

There just wasn't much chemistry established with any of these characters to make me care at all. They felt so focused on their own things that by the time they get together it feels awkward and short lived.

Honestly wished Ormon got more screen-time because he could've been an awesome villain character. Instead he's limited to the beginning and brought back out of nowhere towards the end. Would've been interesting to see him continuing his crime spree and being wreckless, but that's cut short unfortunately.

Nick Notle also had this really bad old man's voice which made it so hard to understand what he was saying. Think of Christopher Walken and Christopher Lyod talking at the same time. It's so bad! He too is wiped out of the story.

The ending was a wet fart too, I thought it was going to be this spectacular ending after planning for this giant heist. Instead we get people yelling in a room and screaming this:

PUT THE GUN DOWN!

PUT THE GUN DOWN!

PUT THE GUN DOWN!

They said this for almost a minute and it was hilariously bad.

The whole happy ending felt forced as well, like Davis just met this officer who so happend to want the car he owned. Sure, why not?

Definitely wait to watch this on streaming.

raw_payload.author: filmtravel101
raw_payload.content:
I really was looking forward to this film as I am a big fan of both director writer Bart Layton and writer Don Winslow but wow this film looks fantastic, great dp work and good acting - but unfortunately the script is terrible.

This film had all the elements of a classic like Heat but it is written like an AI rip off of Heat that has no depth to the characters or believable heist story.

Chris Hemsworth goes from being a clever ocd perfect thief to suddenly he becomes a sloppy inept lame thief that in the final scene

I was waiting for some cat/ mouse twist but it does not exist.

And Mark Ruffalo character is even more pathetic and poorly written.

Why would he steal the diamonds and then give them to Halle Barry. Huh?

There is no build up of any connection for this bizarre lame twist. It ruins the entire film.

The finale is the most laughable that Mark Ruffalo gets the cool oldie muscle car to tie in the writers love of Steve McQueen nostalgia films

The morale of the story is good cops let non violent thief's get away.

That was the beauty of Heat that good cops never let a thief get away - ever. This twist is unrealistic and amateurish.

This film had so much potential but the 2 writers could not come up with any clever ideas for the characters to be believable, and the story just drags on with no heist substance for a real payoff.

Great effort yet it leaves one yawning instead of feeling like it's a great heist film.

Title should be Dumb and Dumber Heist 101

5 stars for DP work, actors and cool LA shots.

raw_payload.author: kgprophet
raw_payload.content:
You could call this entry to the heist genre "Michael Mann-Lite". With the brooding synthesiser soundtrack keeping the tense undertone, we can't but help to make comparisons to "Heat" or "Thief". And since that comparison seems to be the aim of the filmmakers, we can't help but make a judgement of whether this yarn measures up to Mann's trend setting films. The answer is 'kinda'. I like the source material. It is a great set of main characters to cook up a plot. Mark Ruffalo by far takes the center focus of this group, even though Chris Hemsworth is playing the Robert De Niro character. Halle Berry has some strong moments as someone who has paid her dues but ends up being snubbed. It could be there is some reflection of Halle's own career during those scenes where she clashes with her colleagues. But the most charismatic character is the 'bad' bad guy. I first saw Barry Keoghan as a supporting character in "Dunkirk". His understated performance was memorable. He has since shown up in a number of good movies and mini-series. He manifests a very dangerous personality in this film as a reckless young beginner in the robbery business.

Hemsworth as the master thief does not feel like a good fit. I still think of him as "Thor" or the crazy evil leader in "Furiosa". A self assured braggart who is battle ready. Instead, Hemsworth must withdraw all those flamboyant character traits to personify an introverted and socially awkward loner. It is a mismatch that left me wondering who might have been a better fit. For one, it is a little strange that all these hunky American characters are filled by Brits or Aussies. From Mel Gibson (Lethal Weapon), to Russell Crowe and Guy Pearce (L. A. Confidential), to Gerard Butler (Den of Thieves). Jeremy Renner would be a for instance, if it were not for his horrible accident. Perhaps an introverted Rami Malik?

This is based on a novella, a short story, by a crime story author. By observing the structure of this story, I could see this as a good read. But the transition into a screenplay was not very successful. For such a small set of characters (our master thief has no crew), the movie drags far too long beyond the source material. With many extended scenes of love interests spelling out their relationships, the thinness of the material starts to show. As much as the love interest is integral to the heist template, the amount of screen time shouldn't be more than time spent on the caper itself.

As for the heists themselves? It seems for such immensely valuable items being transported, the security is really small. Just one bodyguard or a couple people on guard when walking around with millions in goods? Can such elaborate heists be pulled off without a crew? Helmsworth employs a hacker, but he is not a full time member. So unfortunately, despite the grounded approach to the subject, plausibility is strained.

Ultimately the movie is buoyed by Ruffalo, channelling the beloved detective Columbo. A lovable and wise career cop that is set in his old ways. He helps to deliver an engaging third act as the big heist goes down. But the big payoff is not really worth a 2+ hour slog to get there. I think a good trim would have given this film better momentum.

raw_payload.author: evan_harvey
raw_payload.content:
I think we need to come to terms with the fact that Chris Hemsworth cannot act.

This film both sucks and blows. It's derivative, boring and stupid. Chris Hemsworth plays an experienced criminal who seems rattled by violence, which makes no sense. Presumably this is because he grew up in foster care, but, like, he's an experienced criminal who engages in high adrenaline crimes. But he's actually a really great guy, who doesn't like violence.

He also appears to be mentally challenged when talking to women. Fair enough, it can be hard, but you'd expect that an experienced criminal would probably have a certain level of confidence.

It seems like it's trying to be a bit of a character study, but fails to build any characters or study them.

So after you slog through all the boring parts, which is 90% of the film, the ending really sucks.

Why does the cop decide to steal the diamonds? Why does he give them to Halle Berry? Why does Hemsworth give his car to the cop?

Skip it. It's not worth it.

raw_payload.author: slightlymad22
raw_payload.content:
I just got out of Crime 101.

This was not what I expected. The trailers (which gave away some huge plot points, especially in the final act) did nothing for me, and I thought it looked like another Amazon streaming movie filled with Marvel actors (collecting big pay checks and phoning in performances). But the girl I'm seeing thinks Chris Hemsworth is very easy on the eyes, so I went with her lol

It is a real slow burn, I thought this was going to be around 90 minutes, but I ended up spending about 3 hours in the cinema.

I have to say I had a surprisingly good time with it. It has a really strong opening, no opening credits (which I appreciate) The performances are all really good, there was not a weak link in the cast, also a few surprising faces show up (Jennifer Jason Leigh and Paul Adelstein) it is very well directed (I even enjoyed the chase scene's, which everyone knows bore me now) does a good job with the ebbs and flows of it's storylines and the cinematography is absolutely gorgeous, it's very well shot.

Whilst I felt its run time, I was surprisingly invested in these people's lives. They are all generally good people, in their respective lives, who do some things that are questionable come the end of the movie.

It is a lot like Heat, but without the shootouts, the core characters are essentially lifted from Heat.

Hemsworth = DeNiro a detached professional, who lives in an empty apartment at the beach, who embarks on a relationship Ruffallo = Pacino an obsessed cop whose private life is crumbling Nolte is Jon Voight the handler Barry Keoughan is not exactly like Waingro, but I couldn't think of anyone else who was a psychotic killer in Heat

Halle Berry's character is the difference. Nobody in Heat has a cleavage that great!!

Of the cast, Hemsworth does well as the Anti Hero (thief with a code of honour) Mark Ruffalo is great as the hard boiled detector, looking a lot like Columbo; Halle Berry looks great (so natural) and she has a great role, which gives her something to do. Barry Keoghan does well, playing an unhinged an unpredictable character.

But I think it's too long (I don't know what I'd cut, though), there are too many plot conveniences, the finale was a tad predictable (I knew how it would end an hour in) and a bit more action wouldn't have went amiss, either.

All in all, Bart Layton directed a really enjoyable movie, that I had a good time with. It's currently my favourite movie of the year.

raw_payload.author: stevencsmovies
raw_payload.content: When is enough truly enough? Contrary to my belief, CRIME 101 is not a class or textbook of how to do crime, but the name of a new crime thriller written and directed by Bart Layton. Based on a novella by Don Winslow, this is a heist movie that knows what it's doing. Mike (Chris Hemsworth) is a gentlemanly, meticulous jewel thief who works for Money (Nick Nolte), the object and the guy. Another guy who works for him is Ormon (Barry Keoghan), a psychotic biker wildcard. Sharon (Halle Berry) is an insurance broker who is striving for a promotion that keeps getting out of reach. Lou (Mark Ruffalo) is a detective who's convinced these robberies on the 101 highway are connected. He's also going through a divorce with Angie (Jennifer Jason Leigh). Maya (Monica Barbarro) is Mike's love interest, who tends to ignore a few too many red flags (I'd probably ignore them too if it was Hemsworth). This movie is great at just letting these fully established characters play in the playground that is Los Angeles. They each have clear motives and talented actors giving it their all. This movie dives deep into the theme of class, how money has a real influence on how you live your life and your morality. This is a throwback to movies like HEAT. As in most movies with Barry Keoghan, he is a wildcard wrench in the gears in the most exciting way possible. I was invested in the drama of these characters' lives. There are great chase sequences and very tense scenes. Ruffalo plays a very similar role in this as he did in the series TASK. I really enjoyed how smoothly we would transition from character to character. It might be a little too long for some, but I was just happy being driven as a passenger for as long as they had me. This movie cost too much money, so people will probably call it a flop, which is sad. Overall, CRIME 101 is a slick, entertaining drive around Los Angeles that accelerates faster and faster as it goes on. I highly recommend getting in this ride. Just remember your seatbelt.

raw_payload.author: oNerdcall
raw_payload.content: Crime 101 is a crime thriller that relies on style, a strong cast, and a 90s-inspired atmosphere to carry its narrative. There are solid action sequences, especially the car chases, which bring bursts of energy and tension. However, the film gets lost in an overstuffed structure, focusing more on setting things up than truly developing its characters. The interwoven storylines dilute the emotional impact and lead to a predictable, anticlimactic finale. Despite the cast's charisma and some well-executed action, the script promises more than it delivers. It works as moderate entertainment, but ultimately falls short of its potential.

raw_payload.author: eoinageary
raw_payload.content:
An elusive thief, eyeing his final score, encounters a disillusioned insurance broker at her own crossroads. As their paths intertwine, a relentless detective trails them hoping to thwart the multi-million dollar heist they are planning.

Solid robbery movie. Davis has the final heist of 11 Million all in, after a bust up with an organiser, he is followed by Ormon who is more wreck less than him and wants to take his score.

The pacing of the movie is great at the start, hit about a 20 minute lull and it picks up, it balances the right amount of characters so it's not too weighed down, the soundtrack and visuals are good too.

Acting from Hemsworth is probably strongest with Berry also strong. Not a predictable ending which I like, decent watch at the cinema or at home, good car chases, 7/10.

raw_payload.author: darkreignn
raw_payload.content:
"Crime 101" is the latest in what seems to be a trend of character-driven, over two-hour-long crime thrillers; the recent "Den of Thieves: Pantera" and 2018's "Dragged Across Concrete" are two examples that come to mind and, of course, one can't forget the film that started it all, Michael Mann's "Heat." "Crime 101" follows in these footsteps by, indeed, being slow burn and character-driven - if you're in the mood for a breezy, 90-minute action film, this isn't it. That said, I admire filmmakers who are confident enough in their film to let scenes breathe and trust their characters to carry the weight, and director Bart Layton is certainly confident, working with a smart script, taut editing, and perhaps most importantly, excellent actors. The result is a high-quality, thoughtful, and stylish dramatic crime thriller with heart, brains, and just enough tension to keep you hooked.

I liked this movie a lot, but I didn't love it. Why? I can't really answer that question because, on paper, this has everything I should love. From a soundtrack that reminded me of the movie "Drive," which is my all-time favorite film, to actors whom I adore in the starring leads, to clever intersecting plot lines and slick direction and editing, to intense, white-knuckle heist sequences, "Crime 101" has a lot to keep viewers engaged. Chris Hemsworth is the standout here, giving a performance that is both familiar and fresh; on one hand, he plays the smart, slick, handsome criminal who every man wants to be and every woman wants to be with. He's meticulous, calculated, and looks damn good in a suit, which seems to be his attire of choice on the job. On the other hand, he is (and I found this to be extremely relatable!) socially awkward around women. I can't remember the last time I saw a crime thriller where the main character wasn't as slick as 007 when it comes to the art of seduction, but it was an interesting choice that added layers.

The film builds on that Michael Mann-inspired foundation with long, patient scenes and character development over constant action, but it doesn't quite reach the emotional or philosophical heights of "Heat" or even "Drive's" quiet melancholy. There are many interesting character moments - Mark Ruffalo, Barry Keoghan, and Halle Berry are a pleasure to watch on screen, delivering strong work - and the intersecting stories are clever and engaging with the pacing rewarding patience with payoff in the finale.

I'd be remiss if I didn't speak on the action - there isn't a ton of it in "Crime 101," which may surprise people expecting wall-to-wall shootouts or constant chases, but what's here is definitely thrilling and really well directed. Layton knows how to build suspense, so the film alternates beautifully between slow-burn tension and explosive payoff.

Still, something held it back from being a total knockout for me. I can't really pinpoint it, but I wanted to love it more, but I did like it a lot and will say it's definitely worth watching if you're into thoughtful crime thrillers that take their time.

raw_payload.author: KingKava
raw_payload.content:
I honestly don't understand the hype around this film. The first 15 minutes build a bit of intrigue... and then absolutely nothing happens.

For a movie that constantly talks about a legendary thief pulling off high-level heists along the 101 freeway, you'd expect at least one proper heist scene. There isn't. Not one. It's just endless conversations about crimes that supposedly happened off-screen.

The pacing is painfully slow. It feels like it's building toward something big the entire time, but that payoff never comes. The story drags, and the ending makes no sense whatsoever.

Why does Mark Ruffalo's character suddenly steal the diamonds?

Why give them to Halle Berry?

Why would Chris Hemsworth randomly hand over his car to Ruffalo?

None of it is properly explained or earned. The motivations feel forced, and the whole thing comes across as "mysterious" instead of coherent.

It tries to be smart and subtle, but it just ends up being dull and confusing. If you're expecting tension, clever twists, or actual heists - you won't find them here.

Terrible, boring movie. Save your time.

raw_payload.author: TheMovieSearch
raw_payload.content:
Crime 101 is a solid crime drama led by Chris Hemsworth, Halle Berry, and Mark Ruffalo, delivering an intense, action-packed story filled with robberies, police pursuits, and layered character dynamics. From the start, the film establishes a gripping atmosphere, pulling viewers into a high-stakes world where criminals, law enforcement, and an unsuspecting insurance agent collide. Barry Keoghan also brings his usual unpredictable energy, adding an edge that keeps certain scenes lively and engaging.

The film thrives on its central dynamic between the robbers. One operates with a strange sense of restraint, reluctant to cause harm, while the other leans into instability and danger. This contrast creates compelling tension and opens the door for character development that elevates the material beyond a standard heist film. The screenplay provides enough depth to explore these personalities while maintaining forward momentum through action sequences and dramatic confrontations.

Visually and structurally, Crime 101 offers polished filmmaking. The robbery sequences are tightly staged, and the police chases carry urgency and weight. There's a clear effort to give the story texture, blending suspense with emotional undertones. However, the film occasionally becomes overly dialogue-heavy, slowing its pacing in moments that could have benefited from sharper editing.

At two and a half hours, the runtime feels excessive. While the story is engaging, it doesn't quite justify its length. Certain scenes linger longer than necessary, creating stretches where momentum stalls. Instead of building toward a powerful climax, the film chooses a subdued emotional ending that feels anticlimactic compared to the intensity promised throughout.

Despite these flaws, the performances remain strong across the board. Hemsworth commands the screen with physical presence, Berry adds grounded emotional depth, and Ruffalo brings a steady intensity. Keoghan's involvement injects unpredictability, reinforcing the film's darker tones. Their collective performances help sustain interest even when the pacing falters.

Overall, Crime 101 is an entertaining and competently made crime thriller that delivers strong action and layered character work. However, its extended runtime and uneven pacing prevent it from being truly exceptional. It's a fun watch with memorable moments, but not one that demands a revisit. While it succeeds in many areas, it ultimately feels longer than necessary, leaving viewers engaged yet slightly exhausted by the end.

raw_payload.author: ian-chandler-24
raw_payload.content:
By Ian Chandler - February 19, 2026

Crime 101, written and directed by Bart Layton, is a crime thriller that is heavily reminiscent of Michael Mann's Heat and Collateral. Despite its familiarity with those films, this movie has its own tricks in the duffel bag as well as a packed ensemble, including Chris Hemsworth, Halle Berry, and Mark Ruffalo. So, with a cast like that and a budget of $90 million, expect a loud, frantic caper with many shootouts and... I'm kidding. This is a character-driven slow burn, full of atmosphere and tons of dialogue--which is very welcome in a generation that is addicted to dopamine. With it targeting a more cerebral direction than adrenaline, Crime 101 is a meaty, enthralling movie, wrapped in a shiny, sleek package.

Perhaps the film's greatest risk--and its biggest payoff--is its willingness to hold back on blood pumping, but mindless action scenes. Though it may drag for some, Crime 101's restrained nature allows the tension to rise gradually. With the suspense boiling slowly, it gives the film plenty of room to investigate its four primary characters, most notably Mike Davis. Played by Chris Hemsworth, Mike is a refreshing departure from other criminal protagonists. Yes, he has the no-killing rule and street-smarts; but he is also an incredibly awkward man. Where Baby Driver's Miles and the lead in Drive are largely silent, Mike talks a lot, just in a seclusive, uninviting way--he basically hides in his sunglasses and clean suits. Searching for Mike is Detective Lou Lubesnick, played comfortably by Mark Ruffalo. Though Ruffalo as the cop character is nothing new, this thriller focuses less on his detective skills, favoring more personal details. In fact, the movie is fixed on personal portraits of its characters more so than what they do on screen. Sharon, played by Berry, is the heart of the film, as she so happens to be an insurance agent caught in the heat of the story. Keoghan's Ormon, conversely, is a cold degenerate, engulfed by expletives and aggression that drastically enflames the narrative. His presence is appropriately unwelcome and gives an otherwise smooth story some jagged encounters.

With a more intimate direction, Crime 101's structure gleefully shifts from standard fare to a worthwhile, beefy feature film. Layton trusts the audience to keep up, as much of the film's consequences and setups are off screen, placing the psychological responses at full center. Don't get it twisted: there are a handful of crime scenes that do crank it to eleven; but the writers know that we have seen these kinds of scenes before. So, the film places the cat-and-mouse game up close instead of afar. The stakes feel visceral despite admittedly not shaking off the fact that the characters are played by blockbuster veterans. Even the city itself feels like a character, with the gleaming cinematography and moody music tightening its grasp of the people inside it. It is shot on location too, removing any burden of odd buildings and unnecessary green screens. With the grounded writing and authentic scenery in a blender, Crime 101 has an incredibly immersive, cinematic texture.

Additionally, Crime 101 scores a tactile, weighty 2000s-era aesthetic that is sorely missed. Minus the fancy new technology, the film feels like a 4K rescan of a movie from 20 years ago, and that is a good thing. There are few silly quips, just one social media reference, and a bigger emphasis on face-to-face conversations, distinguishing Crime 101 from other modern pictures. But there is no escaping it; the film embraces Michael Mann's style to the point where it is a little reliant on his blueprints. If the film tried to be more different, the comparisons to Heat would not be pervasive. Perhaps a more outlandish (but well designed) twist or a spin on the formula might have made Layton's film a more distinguished effort; but alas, what we get is still a great, Mann-esque homage.

Despite its obvious influences and few bursts of innovation, Crime 101 is one of the first great movies of 2026. Its preference for realistic robberies and simmering tension drive it to be an engaging, somewhat challenging picture that is worth seeing on the big screen. It is also simply enjoyable to see some stars, most prominently Hemsworth and Ruffalo, be in more projects outside of the superhero spectrum again. If you miss how fun these movies were over a decade ago, then this will scratch the itch.

Score: 79/100

Summary (for quick reference): With a great script and grounded setting, Crime 101 knows how to be cool while staying in its lane. And while it is no masterwork, the film is a quality offering that makes you miss when these heist movies were in their heyday.

raw_payload.author: Grumpyzz
raw_payload.content:
I had a really good time with Crime 101, a slick and stylish noir crime thriller that absolutely delivers a great popcorn flick.

The big initial pull factor here for me was this star studded cast and they absolutely delivered. Chris Hemsworth was the standout, playing a bit of an awkward but complex character that has layers to peel back on as the strong lead protagonist. Mark Ruffalo also has a compelling character here with a whole arc of his own to follow in an effective and memorable grounded performance, making his time on the screen always a strong point of this movie. The transitions here throughout are smooth and sharp, giving the film a fluid momentum that keeps you locked in from scene to scene especially in its first act which had me hooked from the jump.

That said, the narrative can feel a bit unfocused at times especially in its second act. There are a lot of moving pieces here with many subplots, shifting perspectives, and a stacked lineup of characters, and while it makes the world feel layered, it occasionally comes off a little clustered. You can feel the ambition here but you can also feel that the film doesn't always have enough breathing room to fully maximize every plot idea it introduces.

Where it really comes back together is in the third act. The way the different plot lines weave back into that final stretch is satisfying to watch unfold. While this finale may not take any big risks and ends in a pretty safe way, it still absolutely stuck the landing for me and will please the audience who ended up falling for these characters.

The film starts strong, ends strong, and carries a confident energy throughout. I just wish it had a little more time to really dig into its ideas and let certain moments land even harder and give certain characters more story. Even with that, Crime 101 delivers a stylish and engaging ride that left me pretty satisfied overall.

raw_payload.author: movieman6-413-929510
raw_payload.content:
Crime 101 is a new crime thriller directed, written, and partially produced by Bart Layton, the director of American Animals and The Imposter.

Davis (Chris Hemsworth) is an elusive thief in Los Angeles, constantly slipping through the hands of the police. Detective Lou (Mark Ruffalo) is determined to finally catch him, even as his own personal life is falling apart. For his latest heist, Davis crosses paths with Sharon (Halle Berry), a woman working in the insurance world who is taken less and less seriously by her company due to a string of less successful days.

The two may be able to help each other pull off a multimillion-dollar robbery, but whether they can truly trust one another remains uncertain. If Davis fails to succeed in the heist, other criminals higher up have assigned the more brutal criminal Ormon (Barry Keoghan) to take over the job - someone who will handle the mission in a far more ruthless way.

This is an engaging crime thriller that, in terms of storytelling and setting, may remind viewers of the 1995 classic Heat. Both films partly revolve around a cop and a criminal trying to outsmart one another, while also exploring their careers and personal lives. The film is beautifully shot and makes strong use of the Los Angeles setting to serve the story.

The movie does have a slow build-up, introducing the different characters separately at first. Once the characters eventually come together, more collaborations and confrontations arise, forcing them to figure out who they can and cannot trust. This remains compelling to follow as a viewer: which alliances do you personally believe in, and do those turn out to be the right choices for the characters?

The cast delivers strong and fitting performances. Chris Hemsworth portrays a more good-natured criminal who prefers to avoid unnecessary violence, as long as he can successfully carry out his crimes. In his private life, however, he is a more insecure man. Halle Berry plays a woman who, due to her age, is taken less seriously within her company and risks being replaced by a younger employee. Mark Ruffalo plays an older detective going through a divorce and seemingly somewhat out of shape for the job, yet he stubbornly tries to continue in an old-fashioned way, even when this causes friction with younger colleagues.

Through this approach to storytelling, the characters are given depth beyond their careers, allowing the audience to care more about them and better understand the choices they make.

raw_payload.author: masonsaul
raw_payload.content:
Crime 101 is a slickly made heist movie that remixes Heat successfully, mostly knows how to use an impressively star studded cast and resolves everything in a satisfying fashion. It's engaging for every moment of a 140 minute run time which neatly moves between the intersecting lives of its characters and keeps the action to a couple of impactful sequences whilst always maintaining high stakes. There's also a really nice practicality to this LA set crime saga that never goes unnoticed.

Chris Hemsworth plays against type when he looks like he's doing the opposite by having a convincing confidence when it comes to controlling the room in a tense heist and being awkward in every other scenario. Mark Ruffalo excels as an exhausted cop who can't let his hunch go and Barry Keoghan knows how to be a threatening loose cannon. Halle Berry brings an empathetic dissatisfaction to her character so the eventual decision to participate in the heist feels believable.

Writer & director Bart Layton quickly grabs your attention thanks to an opening which efficiently sets things up and culminates with an exciting car chase. Erik Wilson's beautiful yet clinical cinematography is constantly finding striking positions to shoot scenes from, especially anytime the camera is mounted on a vehicle. Blanck Mass delivers a fantastic score that elevates every scene it's used for because the sombre ambience works for any tense situation or quiet moment of reflection.

The story takes place in Los Angeles so Chris uses the 101 freeway to make his escapes, in case you were wondering about the name of the movie. Halle plays an insurance broker that is dissatisfied with her job and is approached by Chris for a big score. It's rated R for language, violence and sexual content-no nudity-and has a running time of 2 hours & 19 minutes.

I enjoyed it and it would be a good one to stream.

raw_payload.author: PerryAtTheMovies
raw_payload.content:
6.7/10 (7) (Recommend)

I wish I could give my rating ok all the sites as a 6.7/10 because it wasn't good enough to be a perfect 7 of a movie, but it wasn't terrible enough to be a mediocre 6. So, for arguments sake, I'm giving it a 7 simply because I was entertained throughout the entire 139 minute run time. This is one of those movies that I would've loved as a teenager, or a young adult. Hang with the boys, go see an action movie, and just generally enjoy myself.

The acting was good. I really enjoyed who played the major rolls. Chris Hemsworth really played his character well. It may have been slightly over obvious at times, but he had the whole paranoia thing down for his criminal. Halle Berry was dynamite. She brought a great spark to the overall of the film. Barry Keoghan was so good I accidentally mistook him for Ezra Miller for most of the film the way he was being kind of schizo-paranoid. Lastly, something about Mark Ruffalo playing characters down on their luck. He plays the average man very well. He always has.

The story may not be new or fresh, but I genuinely enjoyed it. Despite it hitting all the cliches the way it's presented just makes fun and entertaining. It's a well-defined story that hits all the marks. I feel like if you read the book and then watched the movie things would line up very nicely.

Overall, it's an enjoyable movie that can be enjoyed in theatres if you're looking to get out and enjoy some action, drama, and thriller. Come for the story, but stay for the actors. I know I enjoyed the film overall, but a small part of me would still recommend seeing it on a cheap night. It's not a cheap movie, but paying less is more enticing. I'd still see it again, but maybe not in theatres. 139 minutes is long, but still worth it.

That's it for now. Thanks for taking time to read my review. Happy watching and enjoy the show!

raw_payload.author: PWray_99
raw_payload.content: Crime 101 is a crime thriller about an elusive thief trying to do one more score while being chased by the cops. If you have heard that premise before, you know how this story goes. Chris Hemsworth plays the thief with a heart of gold while Mark Ruffalo plays the loser detective who is obsessed with trying to caught Chris Hemsworth. The beginning pace started great but everything slows down to a snail's pace for the middle of the movie as he had to get the introduction of the love interest for Chris. It is introduced in such a dumb way and it doesn't add anything new to the table of "thief starts to get second thoughts because now they have someone they care about". I closed my eyes for a second because I was very bored. The movie tries to slip in quick messages about police corruption and insurance company corruption but doesn't do any substantial that adds to any kind of message. The ending is also way too clean that some characters do 180 arcs with no explanation. It's unfortunate because the cast is good and the cinematography is great. But a mediocre script makes the movie subpar at best.

raw_payload.author: parksiet
raw_payload.content: Crime 101 feels like a crime thriller ripped straight out of the early 90s and I mean that as the highest compliment. It has all the elements I want in a slickly-made, good crime film: car chases, heists, tense standoffs, a firm code of ethics, thieves with hearts of gold, a wild-card villain, decent people caught in bad circumstances they think are beyond their control, until they're not, and more. It starts a bit rocky at first, but by the end you're rooting for these likeable characters to just find a way toward inner peace. I was shocked by how much I fell for this, owning its Michael Mann origins while crafting its own identity with thrilling yet deeply human stories of broken people searching for stability in the wrong ways. Honestly, I'm amazed a solid, original film like this was able to be made in this age of cinema, especially with the stacked cast! The entire cast is excellent, carrying that old-school charm and energy of some classic 90s crime thrillers. While it's a bit too long, Crime 101 is a reminder that engaging action thrillers don't need wall-to-wall visual effects to work. Writer/Director Bart Layton brings a unique touch to a familiar formula and delivers several intense sequences that treat the setting like a character in itself. The dialogue works efficiently, withholding information when necessary, while also tackling a few interesting elements beyond the crimes themselves. Crime 101 is a rock solid crime thriller that excels at utilizing its stacked cast in a variety of ways. It's way better than it has any right to be, and is a really great time at the movies!

raw_payload.author: stevenbarnett-24845
raw_payload.content:
Crime 101 is a slow-burn crime thriller that understands the value of patience. Rather than rushing from plot point to plot point, the film moves with deliberate precision, carefully laying down character motivations and narrative threads until everything clicks into place. That restraint pays off - when the final act arrives, the sense of completion is genuinely satisfying.

One of the film's biggest strengths is how it introduces its characters. Each person enters the story like a distinct piece of a larger puzzle, and the interactions between them unfold organically. You get the sense that these people existed before the camera started rolling, which gives the world a grounded authenticity that elevates the tension.

The performances across the board are strong, but what really stands out is how easy it is to invest emotionally in Davis, Lou, and Sharon. They're written with enough nuance that you find yourself rooting for all of them, even when their goals don't perfectly align. That moral gray area adds depth without sacrificing momentum.

Visually, the film is consistently engaging. The cinematography has a cool, controlled style that complements the methodical pacing, and there's an excellent chase sequence that injects adrenaline at exactly the right moment. The score also deserves credit - it enhances the mood without overpowering scenes, reinforcing the tension simmering beneath the surface.

What ultimately makes the movie memorable is its rewatchability. Knowing how everything connects doesn't diminish the experience; if anything, it invites you to notice the subtle setups and character beats you might have missed the first time.

Rating 8.1.

raw_payload.author: ThereelscoopwithKK
raw_payload.content:
The cast and trailer set high expectations for me, but the movie never came close to delivering on them. Despite the long runtime, the lead characters feel flat, and by the time the climax arrives, I was not nearly invested enough to really care about the outcome.

The one standout performance is Barry Keoghan in a supporting role, who has mastered playing a villainous pest. Halle Berry does her part competently as well, but her character ultimately ends up feeling unnecessary to the story. The writers appeared to be developing momentum between her and Chris Hemsworth's character, only for it to be discarded and the build up all for naught.

The biggest disappointments, however, are Mark Ruffalo and Hemsworth. Ruffalo seems to have lost some of his former vigor, and Hemsworth comes across far too flat for a lead role. I understand they were aiming for a mysterious, silent type, but it doesn't work here. Movies like this need a lead with real on-screen charisma and it's missing here.

In the end, the film is not a total waste. Hardcore fans of the genre are still likely to enjoy it , but in my view there are far better options out there.

raw_payload.author: southdavid
raw_payload.content:
The first movie that Bart Layton has made without some element of documentary to them, "Crime 101" has high ambitions and sees a film like "Heat" as one it's aspiring towards. It nearly gets there too, until the final quarter of the film which, in my eyes anyway, let it down.

LAPD Detective Lou Lubersnick (Mark Ruffalo) cuts a lonely figure expounding his theory that a number of organised robberies along the 101 freeway, are the work of a single thief. That thief is 'Mike' (Chris Hemsworth) whose meticulous planning has meant that the crimes are largely violence free. His fence (Nick Nolte) is a lot less scrupulous though and wants more of the robberies to take place - as a result, he takes a robbery that Mike has dismissed as too dangerous and gives it to Ormon (Barry Keoghan) whose propensity for, and tolerance of, violence is much higher.

This might be one of my rare reviews with a spoiler warning as, as I said above, I think the film is really strong until that last quarter. I liked the performances. It's a subdued turn from Chris Hemsworth, turning down his natural charm for a smaller and more 'fidgety' role. Ruffalo is great, Halle Berry is also in the main cast and is good. Keoghan always has a broken little psychopath in him and does that again here. The story is quite good. . . If perhaps lacking in some originality. Mark Ruffalo's lone good cop against both the criminals, and the corruption in his precinct is well worn, even if I liked seeing him do it.

It's all really good until it comes to a head. Then the film feels painfully unwilling to pick between it's two protagonists, so gives a compromised ending to all the characters involved that is particularly unsatisfying. It's so underwhelming it almost completely invalidates all the slow burn character work that the film has done up to this point.

raw_payload.author: steiner-sam
raw_payload.content:
It's a jewelry heist movie set in modern times in Los Angeles and along the coastal U. S. Route 101. There are five main characters in this clever, but formulaic, heist movie. Mike Davis (Chris Hemsworth) is a top-end jewelry thief working for a fence known as Money (Nick Nolte). Ormon (Barry Keoghan) is a young motorcycle-riding psychotic who also works for Money. Sharon Combs (Halle Berry) is an overlooked insurance executive. Police Detective Lou Lubesnick (Mark Ruffalo) is the jaded, but honest, cop who is convinced a single person has committed a series of jewel robberies along Route 101. Maya (Monica Barbaro) is a blue-collar worker who hits Mike's car from behind and ends up as his girlfriend.

"Crime 101" is suitably complex and entertaining as the various characters interact until a climactic confrontation that includes a crooked billionaire (Tate Donovan). Two car/motorcycle chases do not match Steve McQueen in "Bullitt," even though that movie is referenced. The chase scenes felt too gimmicky. The storyline is crisply moved forward, and keeps the viewer current with the unfolding plot, but the ending is facile, with some serious logical problems, e.g., the billionaire's security arrangements and Ormon's sources of information in the movie's latter third. Hemsworth, Ruffalo, Keoghan, and Berry do well in their roles. Worthwhile for fans of heist movies.

raw_payload.author: antoniotierno
raw_payload.content:
Crime 101 is one of those rare crime thrillers that feels both classic and completely fresh at the same time. From the opening scene, the film pulls you into a world of calculated moves, quiet menace, and razor-sharp intelligence. It doesn't rely on cheap twists or exaggerated action - instead, it builds suspense methodically, rewarding patient viewers with a gripping and deeply satisfying experience.

The screenplay is exceptionally tight. Every line of dialogue feels purposeful, and the cat-and-mouse dynamic at the heart of the story is handled with remarkable restraint. Rather than spelling everything out, the film trusts its audience to pay attention - and that confidence elevates the entire experience. The pacing is deliberate but never slow; it simmers, then strikes exactly when it should.

The performances are outstanding across the board. The lead delivers a nuanced, layered portrayal that balances charisma and danger effortlessly. Supporting characters are equally compelling, each adding texture and tension to the unfolding narrative. The chemistry between rivals is electric, making every confrontation feel meaningful and unpredictable.

Visually, the film is sleek and atmospheric. The cinematography enhances the mood with sharp contrasts and carefully composed frames that mirror the precision of the characters themselves. The score is subtle yet effective, heightening tension without overwhelming the scenes.

What truly sets Crime 101 apart is its intelligence. It respects the genre's roots while adding a modern edge, resulting in a thriller that feels both timeless and contemporary. It's stylish without being flashy, cerebral without being inaccessible, and intense without being over the top.

For fans of smart crime dramas, Crime 101 is a must-watch. It's gripping, elegant, and leaves a lasting impression long after the credits roll.

raw_payload.author: alibabs123
raw_payload.content:
A real cool crime thriller with a deliberate, slow-burn feel. Hemsworth does well to get the balance of a slick, professional thief and a shy, almost diffident soul, looking for that final payday to get him out of this life. A 'two-sides of the coin' theme that runs through the storys' DNA.

The Los Angeles backdrop - the tent-littered sidewalks that highlight the all too real poverty and homelessness crisis that is so prevalent in a city synonymous with the rich a famous.

The moral compass of even the most straight-laced of characters when faced with the choice of what's right vs what's expected.

The blurring of the lines - who are the real criminals here?

Modern German engineering or American muscle!?

What I think the film lacks, is a real big 'heist' set piece. However, by the very nature of how methodical and subtle the characters go about their work, it wouldn't fit the narrative. So however much an iconic bank-heist/street shootout would have elevated Crime 101, so it can be in the conversation with Heat or The Town, it's probably best not having one.

There's some great supporting turns too from Barry Keoghan's disruptive pretender-to-the-throne, Halle Berry's Insurance executive with a conscience and most notably Mark Ruffalo's grizzled, by-the-book detective with a point to prove...and every 'De Niro' needs his 'Pacino'.

raw_payload.author: Rade_M
raw_payload.content:
Surprisingly solid character driven heist film.

I went in with zero expectations and ended up genuinely enjoying Crime 101. It's marketed as a heist thriller, but it works much better as a story about three people stuck in systems that use their talents without ever recognizing them. The film trusts the audience to understand their choices without overexplaining, which makes the characters feel more grounded.

Hemsworth, Ruffalo and Berry all deliver strong, understated performances, and their arcs come together in a way that feels earned. Barry Keoghan steals every scene he's in and brings exactly the energy the film needs at the right moment.

The plot is believable and well paced, though the film leans a bit too much on familiar archetypes. Even so, it's far more human and engaging than I expected.

A pleasant surprise. Recommended.

raw_payload.author: redban02
raw_payload.content:
I went to see Crime 101 on 02/19/2026. I didn't like it so much

There are a few upsides. It's a crime/mystery/thriller; and maybe a couple moments are exciting or tense, such as the few heist scenes and the hotel-room finale. I enjoyed Mark Ruffalo's scenes the most. I think he does well as an unkempt police officer (reviews have compared him, accurately, to Columbo). The movie does a fine job in presentating Los Angeles faithfully. It's not the city of sunny skies and idylic beaches. The city looks drab, with tons of homeless. I like the drone shots of the highway.

I have so many issues though -- I went into this movie relatively blind, having skimmed the synopsis and one review .. So I guess I was expecting Heat, Collateral, or Den of Thieves, which this movie is not.

This movie is more mystery than action. With all the subplots dangling all over the place, the first half actually reminded me of Crash: there's the main heist storyline between Ruffalo and Hemsworth, but there's stuff with Halle Berry's workplace, Hemsworth and Nick Nolte, Hemsworth and the crazy motorcyclist villain, Hemsworth and his romance interest. Some of those plot threads could've been condensed or eliminated

As I said, I like Mark Ruffalo's scenes, but at the same time, I feel like I've seen him play this same character more than once (e.g. The TV series "Task." The first "Now You See Me, Now You Don't" movie).

Halle Berry is ok, but her story, involving her workplace issues, often breaks the flow. It seems like her role was supposed to be the romance interest for Ruffalo, but they couldn't go all the way because of poor physical chemistry between them. So we end up with an awkward character relationship

Nick Nolte doesn't appear as tough and scary as his character needed. Barry Keogan's character is such a forgettable and generic bad-guy; his acting in the finale is cringy

Chris Hemsworth's character is bland, with no personality or memorable traits. I truly don't know why the girl entered an intimate relationship with him (other than she found him hot, I guess?). The circumstances in which they hook up are so contrived too

Hemsworth's character is supposed to this perfect criminal who commits the perfect crimes, but the audience doesn't see it. In the few heists that appear on-screen, things go wrong infact. We're supposed to accept that Hemsworth is so good just cause the cops say that he's pulled off some impressive heists preceding the events of the film

4.5/10.

raw_payload.author: kavindujayawardena
raw_payload.content:
Crime 101 is nothing short of a masterpiece. From the very first scene, the film grips you with razor-sharp storytelling, layered characters, and an atmosphere charged with tension. It's rare to find a crime thriller that balances intelligence, emotion, and suspense so perfectly - but Crime 101 does exactly that.

The screenplay is brilliantly structured, unfolding like a strategic chess match where every move matters. Each twist feels earned, not forced, and the narrative keeps you constantly guessing without ever losing clarity. The pacing is flawless - not a single scene feels unnecessary.

The performances are phenomenal. Every character carries depth, complexity, and moral ambiguity, making the conflicts feel real and intense. The chemistry between the leads elevates the psychological battle at the heart of the film.

Visually, the cinematography is sleek and immersive, enhancing the dark, high-stakes tone. Combined with a powerful score, the film creates an experience that is both thrilling and emotionally resonant.

Crime 101 doesn't just entertain - it sets a new benchmark for crime dramas. Smart, stylish, and unforgettable.

raw_payload.author: filmreviewradical
raw_payload.content: Jewel 'thief' Mike (Chris Hemsworth) does his well planned crimes up and down Route 101 in California and tries to rope in a disgruntled insurance broker called Sharon (Halle Berry), whilst a crumpled sub-Columbo detective (Mark Buffalo) pursues him. Writer/director Bart Layton's 2026 released Los Angeles set feature film crime thriller (based on the novel by Don Winslow) asks the question 'What will people do for money?', especially when coupled with dodgy insurance companies and corrupt police departments, and like any reasonable thriller also asks the question 'Who are the real criminals?'. Not bad as a thriller, if rather unspectacular and not entirely original seeming, it has a cast which also includes Monica Barbaro, Nick Nolte and Barry Keoghan (as the story's fly in the ointment), and an idea of love and happiness as a cure for the ills of money, materialism and lies.

raw_payload.author: ENG-ORGANO
raw_payload.content:
Rarely does a film command your senses like Crime 101.

I was so mesmerized by its seamless rhythm that I abandoned my snacks and drinks, unwilling to blink for fear of losing a single moment of its brilliance.

Davis (James) is a captivating enigma.

By keeping his past a mystery, the film forces us to embrace his present-a man in a relentless war with his own violent instincts, finding a fragile sanctuary in Maya.

Parallel to him is Lou, a detective trapped in a loop of principles that cost him his marriage and social standing.

He is a man who seeks solitude, not just to be alone, but to find his soul.

The film's true power lies in its social mirrors: Sharon fights for a future built on corporate illusions, only to realize the "system" is more cold-blooded than any thief.

The messages are haunting: "I will pass by you, but you won't care." It's a profound exploration of how we are all connected yet utterly isolated.

The ending is a stroke of cinematic genius.

When Lou grants Davis his freedom, it's an act of "spiritual justice."

He chose the man of code over the man of chaos.

It proves that you don't have to be a friend to recognize a kindred spirit.

A complete, breathtaking experience that satisfies every bit of curiosity without needing a single flashback.
