raw_payload.author: Spielberg_Fan_
raw_payload.content:
I found Crime 101 to be a very well-made Film that has many layers to each character, while keeping me guessing what will happen in the end and ultimately leaving me with a very rewarding conclusion.

The set up for this film does take a while to unfold, but I did think that the director let each character develop nicely as well as letting the film breathe and have a steady pulse.

Mark Ruffalo definitely has a low-key grind it out detective vibe to his character that I can appreciate with normal everyday life character flaws such as a troubled marriage and going to yoga and trying to stay healthy to get back in shape. I thought most people could relate to his character while also root for him to catch the bad guy or the antagonist to the story, Chris Hemsworth's character. Both of these actors do a great job of acting work in my opinion. Their combined effort in the film is authentic to me, and all leads up to a satisfying conclusion that makes sense for each character. Both of their chemistry at one of the final scenes in the movie becomes heart-pounding (and kept me guessing) starting in a car ride and then progressing on to the end result of the main plot.

Chris Hemsworth's character fits the part of the main criminal that Mark Ruffalo's character is trying to catch during this whole film. After the initial set up of movie, we learn that Hemsworth's character has actual deep feelings that has relatable emotional depth to it. His character actually cares for people while not wanting to hurt or injure them even though he is ultimately robbing them and getting away with money and the stolen goods. Chris Hemsworth does a great job of playing the criminal part (bad ass sexy bad guy) and also becomes relatable by caring for a woman he likes and is dating along the way during the middle of the film. This gives him a deep emotional connection to the viewer because it adds layers to his character that left me feeling for him as well as not wanting him to die in the end. His character ends up being a caring human being instead of just playing the criminal role (bad ass bad guy).

Halle Berry's character ends up being trapped in the middle of both of the two main characters stories and has a nice arch to the sub plot of her character's story. I thought she was a great addition as a female supporting actress and character to the whole main plot to the story. She is on her game as far as acting chops go in my opinion and results with me feeling sorry for her character in the end but thankful that she had a positive conclusion to the story.

The handheld camera work was well done during the robbery parts of the film and made that feel authentic and real to me. The cinematography was superb and sharp throughout the film as well as having great editing along the way. The sound was precise and also had a great pace to it that all built up to a heart pounding 20 minutes at the end of the film.

The end result of the story adds up perfectly to me and makes a lot of sense that left me satisfied with a smart, well thought out ending result (conclusion) to the movie.

This is definitely a mild pacing slow burn type of crime film (with a steady pulse) that had me guessing until the end and was definitely worth the watch in theaters.

raw_payload.author: ongoam
raw_payload.content:
Watching Crime 101 in IMAX wasn't just seeing a movie - it was stepping directly into a high-stakes chess match between hunter and hunted.

From the opening frame, the expanded IMAX aspect ratio makes everything feel bigger, sharper, and more immersive. The coastal landscapes look vast and isolating, while the quiet interiors feel claustrophobic in a way that heightens every whispered line and loaded glance. The format doesn't just enhance the visuals - it amplifies the tension.

The story unfolds with a deliberate, confident pace. Instead of relying on explosive spectacle, Crime 101 leans into psychological suspense. Every conversation feels like a duel. Every pause carries weight. The IMAX sound design deserves special praise - subtle ambient noise, distant waves, the click of a firearm - all feel precise and unsettlingly present.

What makes the IMAX experience special here is restraint. When the film does escalate, the impact hits harder because the screen and sound system magnify every detail. It's immersive without being overwhelming, stylish without feeling artificial.

This isn't a loud blockbuster built for IMAX - it's something more refined. The format turns a cerebral crime thriller into an almost tactile experience.

If you get the chance, see it in IMAX. The scale adds intensity in ways that standard screens simply can't replicate.

raw_payload.author: jboothmillard
raw_payload.content: My Mum was excited to see this upcoming movie because of the big names in the cast, especially the star of Thor, so I was happy to see it at the cinema with her and my brother, directed by Bart Layton (The Imposter, American Animals). Basically, in Los Angeles, Mike Davis (Chris Hemsworth) is an elusive and disciplined jewel thief who carefully plans robberies along Route 101; he is never violent and avoids leaving any DNA evidence. Intercepting a diamond delivery carrying decoys, he steals genuine diamonds worth $3 million, but is shaken by a gunshot, and a stray bullet grazes him. His underworld middleman, Money (Nick Nolte), offers him a robbery in Santa Barbara, but Mike calls it off. Instead, Money recruits volatile young biker Ormon (Barry Keoghan). LAPD Detective Lou Lubesnick (Mark Ruffalo) has been linking a string of unsolved robberies along the 101. He suggests that a lone suspect is responsible, but Police Captain Stewart (Matthew Del Negro), his partner, Detective Tillman (Corey Hawkins), and his colleague, Detective Townsend (Drew Powell), are unconvinced. Preparing another heist, Mike pays Devon (Devon Bostick), a hacker, for information on high-end insurance broker Sharon Combes (Halle Berry). Steven Monroe (Tate Donovan) is a wealthy client of Sharon's, but she has long been undervalued by her firm. She is further frustrated when a new colleague closes the deal with Monroe. Ormon violently carries out the Santa Barbara job, which Mike sees on the news. Following this, Mike cuts ties with Money, who orders Ormon to intercept Mike's next heist. Lou continues his search against the wishes of his superior. Lou has a conversation with Sharon because she is a key source of information. Meanwhile, he confronts his unfaithful wife Angie (Jennifer Jason Leigh) in a restaurant and wants a separation. Mike has been lonely for some time, often spending time with escorts. One day, his car is hit by a stranger, PR assistant Maya (Monica Barbaro), who gives her details following the incident. Mike asks her on a date, but she seems uninterested, and he admits he would not have chosen a classy restaurant. They go to a regular restaurant and start to connect, and they begin a relationship. Mike realises he is being tracked by Ormon, who threatens Devon into revealing that Sharon is connected to Mike's plan. Mike tracks and introduces himself to Sharon; he later tries to recruit her as an accomplice in the upcoming heist, but she rejects him. Mike spots Ormon, and they get into a high-speed chase. After surviving a collision, Orson is warned by Mike to stay away. While Lou joins Sharon's yoga class to talk to her, he discovers the car Mike abandoned following the most recent robbery. A trace of blood (from the gunshot) is found, and Mike is traced by his juvenile record; his real name is James Davis. Sharon is denied another promotion, and she changes her mind, agreeing to help Mike. Demanding a $3 million share, she provides inside information on an illicit diamond purchase Monroe has arranged to make with $5.5 million in cash. Monroe is planning his wedding to his young fiancée, Adrienne (Andra Nechita), and will be staying at the Beverly Wilshire Hotel. Lou is suspended for refusing to help cover up the police shooting of another jewel thief. But he continues his investigation, tracking down Mike's foster mother. Ormon viciously interrogates Sharon, who turns to Lou for help and admits everything. After verbally attacking her slimy boss, Mark (Paul Adelstein), she quits her job. Mike tells Maya he will be leaving town, and wary of his secretive nature, she ends their relationship. The briefcase of diamonds arrives with a courier, and Mike takes the place of his security guard. Lou is posing as the courier and is picked up by Mike at the airport. On the journey to the hotel, they talk about Steve McQueen films, including Bullitt and its iconic Chevy Camaro. Ormon, disguised as a hotel employee, also makes his way to Monroe's wedding suite. Mike and Lou deliver the briefcase to Monroe and his fiancée in the suite. Mike seizes the cash at gunpoint, but Lou pulls out a gun, revealing himself as a police officer. Their standoff is interrupted by Ormon, demanding the case. Ormon shoots and wounds Monroe, but Mike kills Ormon before he can shoot Lou. Mike is allowed to escape empty-handed, and Lou forces Monroe and his fiancée, knowing his criminal activities, to support his story as he frames Ormon for Mike's robberies. Lou swaps the decoy stones taken into evidence from Mike's earlier heist with real diamonds from the briefcase. Lou gives the diamonds to Sharon to start a new life. Later, Lou discovers Mike has left him his vintage Chevy Camaro. Mike sends Maya a childhood photo, revealing his true identity, asking her for a second chance. Also starring Peter Banifaz as Ben Haddad, Payman Maadi as Sammy Kassem, Babak Tafti as Ali, Hossein Mardani as Masoud, Deborah Hedwall as Anne, and John Douglas as Grant. Hemsworth gives an enigmatic performance as the mystery man wanting to get out, Ruffalo is terrific as the dishevelled cop on his tail, Berry is good as the businesswoman with information, and Keoghan is splendid as the nasty biker criminal, but I didn't find Barbaro that interesting, and Jason Leigh was pointless as the cop's estranged wife. Apart from the uninteresting romantic subplot, this is a really engaging urban cat-and-mouse slow-burner with carefully planned robberies, some fast chases and a nerve-jangling, edge-of-the-seat climax, a most worthwhile crime thriller. Very good!

raw_payload.author: Jeremy_Urquhart
raw_payload.content:
The jokes about this being re-Heat-ed leftovers or something along those lines are easy to make, but this did feel almost like a remake. It's got the setting and visually things line up quite a bit. As for the characters/casting, Chris Hemsworth = Robert De Niro, Mark Ruffalo = Al Pacino, Nick Nolte = John Voight, Monica Barbaro = Amy Brenneman, and Barry Keoghan = Kevin Gage. The characters do different things, but they all play a similar role in both stories. I'll concede that Halle Berry's character doesn't really line up with anyone from Heat, and Val Kilmer doesn't have a direct comparison either, but still, you can't help thinking about Heat pretty much for all of Crime 101's 140 minutes.

That being said, I enjoyed it for the most part. There's some somewhat hammy and eye-rolling dialogue, and things that feel familiar if you've watched your fair share of heist/thief movies (not just Heat, though that's the easiest one to compare it to), but you get the ingredients right and it's still a satisfying watch. Keoghan is engaging with a limited role, but his character felt like the most shoe-horned in, and his best scene is one of his first, so he sort of just hangs around a bit awkwardly for the rest of the movie.

There are also things about the ending I don't love, but also, lots of the complaints feel like nitpicks. This is a good dad movie. Next time you see your dad, he will tell you he saw this amazing movie called "Crime Something" and he'll be animated about it for about 30 seconds and then he'll never mention it again. The world needs more dad movies, though. And compared to the Jason Statham movie I saw a couple of weeks ago (Shelter; it took me a while to remember the name), this is good dad cinema. I also thought Chris Hemsworth was quite good in this. He's in this weird position where he's one of the most recognizable actors of the past decade or two, but he still feels underappreciated. I guess his range is underappreciated.
